title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

content.title: The Synthetic Muse: AI and Art

texts.A: The emergence of AI-generated imagery is being hailed as the 'democratization of art,' but this is a shallow interpretation. True art is an act of communication between two conscious minds, rooted in lived experience. [37]While some claim that AI is just another tool like the camera, I believe it is fundamentally different because it lacks the 'intent' that defines a human creator.[/37] [39]Furthermore, the way these models are trained—by scraping the work of millions of living artists without consent—is a form of digital colonialism that will eventually bankrupt the creative class.[/39] The art market has traditionally rewarded those who take genuine creative risks—artists who distill personal suffering, cultural memory, and philosophical inquiry into works that endure across generations. No generative algorithm, however sophisticated, has experienced grief, wonder, or moral conflict. When we allow machines to flood the market with imitation, we do not democratize art; we devalue the human investment that makes it matter. Protecting artists' rights is not conservatism; it is the preservation of culture itself.
texts.B: We are currently in a period of 'techno-panic' that mirrors the arrival of photography in the 19th century. Back then, critics claimed the camera would kill painting; instead, it forced painting to become more abstract and expressive. [40]AI will do the same, taking over the labor-intensive tasks of rendering and shading, allowing artists to focus on high-level conceptual work.[/40] [38]I disagree with those who see AI as a threat to human unique creativity; it is a collaborative partner that can suggest visual combinations a human brain would never conceive.[/38] [37]I share the view that the 'artist' is simply moving from the brush to the prompt, and the resulting work is no less valid for its medium.[/37] Every generation has mourned the loss of technical skills made redundant by new tools, yet what emerged was always richer and more varied. The photographic darkroom gave way to Photoshop; the printing press did not kill calligraphy but liberated it from the burden of producing every page by hand. AI is the latest step in this continuous liberation of creative energy from repetitive execution.
texts.C: The ethical debate over AI art often ignores the fact that human artists have always 'borrowed' from their predecessors. No one creates in a vacuum. However, the scale and speed of AI mimicry are unprecedented. [39]I share the concern that the legal system is woefully unprepared to deal with the copyright implications of generative models, which could lead to a world where human artists cannot compete with their own algorithmic shadows.[/39] [40]That said, I am skeptical that AI will lead to an 'evolution' in art; more likely, it will lead to a glut of hyper-realistic, aesthetically pleasing but emotionally vacant content that devalues the very idea of craftsmanship.[/40] Meaningful legal reform is urgently needed—clear frameworks for attribution, compensation, and consent that acknowledge both the transformative potential of the technology and the legitimate rights of human creators. Without such frameworks, we risk a race to the bottom in which original work is systematically devalued and the profession of the artist becomes economically unviable for all but those with independent wealth. The machine can replicate the style, but never the struggle.
texts.D: The power of AI lies in its ability to act as a mirror to our collective unconscious. By analyzing millions of images, it reveals the patterns and biases of our visual culture. [38]I am less convinced than some of my colleagues that this technology is a 'partner' in any meaningful sense; it is a sophisticated mirror, nothing more.[/38] [37]However, I disagree with the pessimistic view that AI-generated work lacks 'intent'; the human who writes the prompt, curates the output, and refines the result is exercising a clear, albeit new, form of artistic agency.[/37] The real danger is not the machine, but the commercial pressure to prioritize 'algorithmic' art that satisfies a data-driven market over the idiosyncratic visions of individuals. The gallery, the museum, and the collector all have a responsibility to resist this homogenizing pressure and actively champion works that emerge from singular human experience. Diversity of voice and perspective is the lifeblood of any vital culture, and it cannot be generated by a model trained on the statistical average of what has come before.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on whether the person using AI can be considered a legitimate artist with 'intent'?
questions.explanation: A argues that AI 'lacks the intent that defines a human creator.' B, however, argues that the 'artist' is simply moving from the brush to the prompt, validating their role.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a different opinion to B regarding AI's role as a 'collaborative partner' in the creative process?
questions.explanation: B calls AI a 'collaborative partner' that conceives new combinations. D is 'less convinced' it's a partner, describing it instead as a 'sophisticated mirror, nothing more.'

questions.question: Which expert shares A's concern about the impact of AI on the livelihood or legal rights of human artists?
questions.explanation: A calls the training of AI 'digital colonialism' that will 'bankrupt the creative class.' C shares this concern, noting the 'copyright implications' and that artists might not be able to compete with 'algorithmic shadows.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the long-term effect of AI on the quality or evolution of art?
questions.explanation: B believes AI will allow artists to focus on 'high-level conceptual work' (an evolution). C is 'skeptical' of this, predicting instead a 'glut' of 'emotionally vacant' content that 'devalues the idea of craftsmanship.'
